data.title: Suncor Energy: The Multi-Decade Oil Sands Opportunity
data.description:
Suncor Energy (NYSE:SU) (TSX:SU:CA) is one of the largest synthetic crude oil sands companies with a market cap of almost $50 billion. The company has continued to set new records with its operations, but it's underperformed from when we last recommended it at the start of the year. The company's continued integrated assets will enable continued shareholder returns.

Suncor Energy Propositioning

Suncor Energy has a long portfolio of low-cost assets along with being one of the most integrated of the midstream oil companies.

The nature of oil sands is that the company has decades of reserve life across its asset portfolio. Traditionally, the largest issue for oil sands has been takeaway capacity, so the company has also built a strong integrated portfolio. The company's impressive portfolio has enabled it to grow shareholder returns with more than 4% dividend.

Suncor Energy has more than 800 thousand barrels/day in upstream production along with 466 thousand barrels/day of refining capacity. The company's production continues all the way downstream to product sales, where it has 570 thousand barrels/day of product sales. Suncor Energy's assets are spread throughout Canada, primarily along with the U.S.

Suncor Energy is continuing to invest heavily in its business, with almost US$4.2 billion in capital expenditures. The vast majority of this cash goes towards oil sands, which is using up almost 50% of its capital expenditures. On top of the company's maintenance expenditures, the company has almost $1.9 billion in economic investments / growth capital.

Versus an almost $50 billion market capitalization, that's roughly 4% of the company's market cap for growth.

Suncor Energy Financial Positioning

The company has a strong position, which it's focused on using to continue driving shareholder returns.

Suncor Energy has a manageable debt maturity profile with US$7.2 billion in total debt. It's a level that the company can comfortably afford, and it's enabled the company to give substantial cash towards shareholder returns. The company has returned >70% of its market cap to shareholders over the decade, driven by both its dividends and repurchases.

Suncor Energy's financials enable the company to continue its returns.

Suncor Energy Shareholder Returns

Suncor Energy remains committed to shareholder returns after achieving its net debt position.

Suncor Energy's goal is to return 100% of excess funds to shareholders via share buybacks. That's based on the company achieving a net debt target of $8 billion with a commitment to 4% annualized growth. Based on a current dividend of more than 4%, that's a more than 8% dividend + growth yield. That's sufficient capital for the company to continue returns.

The company grew to US$5 billion in free funds flow in 2024. That puts the company at an almost 10% free funds flow. The company expects to earn US$700 million more going into next year due to its early achievement of its net debt target along with a continued breakeven reduction. That will enable continued dividends and growth.

The company's aggressive share repurchase rate has enabled it to reduce its outstanding shares by the mid-single-digits annualized. On top of a strong and growing dividend, it's an impressive asset portfolio.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices. The company has worked to improve operations substantially, but the company's profitability is much weaker in a low-price environment. Given the chance of oversupply in the markets along with historic Canadian takeaway capacity issues, that could hurt Suncor Energy's ability to continue returns.

Conclusion

Suncor Energy is a profitable company at the current levels, and it has a commitment to dividends, dividend growth, and share repurchases. That enables double-digit shareholder returns, along with the company's continued investments in growth capital, which will pay off from its impressive portfolio of assets.

The company has been able to reduce its outstanding shares by mid-single digits, saving the company on dividend expenditures. This is enough for the company to continue dividend growth at the same dividend costs. Overall, that commitment to returns makes Suncor Energy a valuable investment. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Domo: It Could Be A Value Trap
data.description:
Domo (DOMO) provides strong capabilities for companies with less software technical talent and for companies that use the most popular data warehouses, such as Amazon Redshift and Snowflake (SNOW). In Snowflake's integration, Domo is especially effective in providing no-code tools to interact with the Snowflake platform. However, Snowflake can enter Domo's market in the future, reducing Domo's differentiation and pricing power. I rate Domo as a hold.

Domo is cloud native

Domo is a BI cloud-native tool. It sells at a much higher price point than Power BI and Tableau, its closer competitors. Domo's advantage is that it was born in the cloud. This makes the company much more agile.

In fact, the main advantage of being born in the cloud is the deployment of updates that affect the whole product. The updates are done quickly and effectively, keeping the capabilities up to date.

Additionally, the company can maintain an equal service level across all companies that use its product. Desktop versions of Power BI and tableau should have much less performance than Domo's full cloud capabilities.

To differentiate from the larger competitors, Domo is betting on no-code capabilities and a large ecosystem of connectors that relate to multiple data warehouses.

More recently, Domo has been competing with Power BI and Microsoft's (MSFT) copilot integration by deploying Domo's agents, called Agent Catalyst. These semi-autonomous LLM agents allow for text-to-code data transformations and outlier detection, among many other automations.

Domo's integration with leading data warehouses enables a no-code, cost-effective workflow. Starting in the query stage and ending in the visualization stage.

To compete, at much higher price points, Domo has been focusing on no-code capabilities, helping the top management create dashboards even if the team doesn't have data transformation expertise.

In fact, no-code is applied at all stages of the BI process. Firstly, in the data transformation workflow, using SQL like computing blocks that help in the visualization and in the structure of data engineering tasks. Secondly, in the dashboard development by generating pre-defined charts with the incoming data. And finally, in app development. Domo's apps have the ability to collect data from their users.

Diving deeper into Domo's cloud native BI offering, the company developed strong integrations with leading data warehouses like Amazon's (AMZN) Redshift and Snowflake.

Indeed, Domo has the ability to query and transform data using Snowflake's structure. This allows Domo's and Snowflake's clients to just use Snowflake's credits for data transformation and Domo's credits for data visualization.

Snowflake functions as a federated database. Therefore, this allows access to data and resources without the need to copy or transfer. This translates into a benefit since those resources within Snowflake do not consume credits.

Link

This is a massive advantage for Domo and Snowflake users. Another important feature in this connection is the ability to use Domo's no-code data transformation workflows directly on Snowflake's data. Again, not consuming Domo's credits but Snowflake's credits, which should be more cost-efficient than Domo because Snowflake's core function is data queries versus Domo's data visualizations.

Despite this, some suggest the Domo and Snowflake connections could be further optimized.

Domo excludes ad-hoc capabilities from its platform, limiting the benefit of Snowflake's ad-hoc query support.

Domo is a below-average solution to layer on top of Snowflake due to its paywall for onboarding materials, confusing user interface, and lack of ad hoc capabilities.

Link

By not using Snowflake's ad-hoc query support, in other words, Snowflake's ability to speed up queries that are not programmed, Domo-Snowflake integration should lead to less optimal query times, affecting the performance of the integration.

Domo shows lower employed capital needs

Snowflake and Domo used together simplify the workflows from data ingestion to data processing. There is always the danger of Snowflake developing its own data visualization tools to capture more growth. It could be interesting to compare both companies' financials to understand the forces affecting the visualization and data processing supply chain.

Looking at the gross margins, Domo shows superior economics. In the YoY period, Domo's gross margins have remained stable, while Snowflake's have decreased. This might be the effect of Snowflake deploying gen AI tools quicker. LLMs need more compute and access to GPUs which are more expensive than the typical CPUs. Domo is still in the process of deploying its gen AI agents. We should monitor how the gross margins develop.

Domo shows good economics from the point of view of capital invested in the business (note that my employed capital calculations consider only the assets that generate the operating profit). In fact, Domo doesn't show meaningful goodwill. Its unearned revenue almost finances all assets. In comparison, Snowflake produces much more gross profit with slightly more capital invested in the business.

Looking at the future, Snowflake is expected to grow much faster than Domo.

On the other hand, Snowflake is 7x more expensive than Domo.

Working with other cloud platforms might become a strong niche

Domo excels when their clients belong to technical industries that are not as equipped with data scientists and data engineers, like healthcare and law.

Working with Filevine, Domo allows Filevine's clients (lawyers and paralegals) to develop their own custom dashboards with no-code functionalities. Additionally, by being cloud native, Domo integration with Filevine should be smoother than other tools like Power BI and Tableau. The competitors also provide no-code tools but should be harder to fully integrate with smaller cloud platforms like Filevine.

The CEO of Filevine, a leading legal technology platform, highlighted the transformative impact of AI on the legal industry, emphasizing the company's innovative strides with products like Chat with Your Case, which efficiently manages vast amounts of structured and unstructured legal data to allow its customers to effectively manage their cases at scale. Filevine enhances its customers' operations with an analytics offering powered by Domo Everywhere. The CEO mentioned that Filevine's impressive retention rate of over 95% climbs to nearly 100% when Domo is integrated, showcasing Domo's vital role in optimizing data-driven decision-making.

Q1 2026

ChatGPT proliferation might turn no-code tools obsolete

With tools like ChatGPT, employees coding skills are massively improved. This means that no-code tools might lose their importance in a ChatGPT-fueled world.

This could reduce Domo's high seat cost or even cause churn as companies migrate to cheaper solutions like Tableau, Power BI, and others.

Another important risk is the entry of larger platforms with more money to invest and higher talent density. For example, Databricks and Snowflake could enter the BI market, creating redundancies in Domo's offering.

Valuation

Domo faces strong competition from other BI tools and the threat of new competitors in its core market. I wouldn't rate Domo's moat as very strong.

My preferred way of valuing tech companies is by using the earnings power framework exposed by author Adam Seessel.

Because tech companies pump the excess gross profit in sales and R&D to protect their moat and fuel their long-term growth, we could try to understand what could be the potential operating profit if Domo was looking only to optimize the next few years profit.

We can compare Domo to more mature Salesforce which produces a 20% operating profit margin. Considering Domo's weaker moat, we could assign a potential operating profit margin of 15% on Domo's revenue.

In other words, Domo could reduce its SGA expenses by 40% and R&D expenses by 20% if it was focused on the next three years, producing an operating margin of 15%.

To account for the company's growth, we could look 3 years in the future, thus calculating the forward PE.

The math leads to a FWD P/E under 20. This could be considered a strong value play using this framework. Companies with strong moats, long growth runaways, and P/E's under 20 or close to 20 are considered strong value plays.

Conclusion

Domo provides a differentiated offering. Its strong integrations with cloud native partner Snowflake add value to the platform. But its seat price and consumption costs are very high compared to the main competitors.

The company looks cheap from an earnings potential perspective. However, this could be a value trap.

Since Domo doesn't have a strong moat protecting the business and could be highly affected by an entry from Snowflake or Databricks in its core market, I'm not considering this company a buy. It's too risky. I rate Domo as a hold.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2055 Lifetime Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • After a tumultuous start to the second quarter, the financial markets regained their footing and provided positive total returns for the full three months.
  • The portfolio delivered a gain and outperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation was the primary factor in the fund's relative strength.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington's new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop. Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lowerquality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation contributed to results, primarily as a result of our decision to maintain an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. An underweight in international stocks also made a small contribution. On the other hand, an overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute terms weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. An overweight in U.S. mid caps, which didn't keep pace with large caps, had a modest, negative effect on results. The fund's absolute return portfolio further detracted, as did underlying manager performance.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors' rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don't attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.

data.title: TransMedics: CEO Is Buying Shares Ahead Of A Potentially Concerning Q3
data.description:
TransMedics (TMDX) concluded a strong first half of 2025, with another beat and raise quarter.

Investors' full attention is now directed towards the third quarter, which includes a seasonal slowdown. Last year, the severity of the slowdown was so extreme, it sent shares to a 65% spiral.

After the recent insider buying by CEO Waleed H. Hassanein, investors are showing confidence, but I urge caution, as a de minimis share purchase relative to existing holdings might be a bit of window dressing ahead of another dreadful Q3.

Introduction

You can be a very happy, or very sad, TransMedics shareholder, depending on when you bought the stock.

If you bought around the August 2024 peaks, you're still more than 30% in the red. On the flip side, if you bought the dip in early 2025, you're up more than double.

The stock is still recovering from a huge let-down last year, though it seems TransMedics is back on its front foot, with consecutive quarters of beats and raises.

I've been covering the stock on Seeking Alpha since early 2024, with broadly a bullish view, though I've gone from 'Hold' to 'Buy' to 'Hold' several times.

Those changes are the result of balancing between the long-term potential and the short-term risk/reward. Let's begin with the long term.

The Long-Term Investment Thesis

Although healthcare is generally a very complex sector to follow, I think the investment thesis in TransMedics is rather simple.

Demand for organ transplants far exceeds supply. Supply, in this case, is not necessarily organ donations, but rather, the ability to utilize them. That is because an organ starts to go bad after a person dies, which means that a 'race against the clock' to transport the organ to the right patient begins.

This race includes many hurdles, including preservation, doctor availability, and transportation bottlenecks. Each of these helps each other, because the longer an organ can be preserved, it leads to better availability of doctors, and a wider transport window.

Enters TransMedics.

The company has a best-in-class preservation product, mainly in liver, and an industry-leading logistics chain, including owned aircraft and pilot arsenal.

Since going commercial, TransMedics is the main driver of liver transplant growth in the US system. It plans to do the same in heart, lung, and kidney over time, and to expand globally, and it recently won key FDA approvals for both lung and heart.

All in all, I see a very strong likelihood the company will deliver on its 10,000 transplant targets in 2028. In 2024, they generated about $118,000 per transplant, and this should grow over time with stronger logistics penetration.

Assuming only gradual improvements in margins, and a slight increase in logistics penetration, we get to $1.2 billion in net sales in 2028, and $230 million in operating profit.

On current valuation, we get to an exit multiple of 18 times EV/EBIT, for a company set to grow over 20%, strong patents, and a proprietary logistic network. To me, if TransMedics delivers, it could easily trade at twice the valuation by 2028.

That said, the path will be volatile.

Strong Second Quarter Set High Bar

Revenue in the second quarter was $157 million, up 38% Y/Y and 10% Q/Q, driven by a 34% increase in product revenue, and a 44% increase in service revenue, of which, $29.8 million came from logistics.

At the organ level, Liver continues to be the dominant contributor to total sales and growth, with $92 million in the quarter, up 51% Y/Y, while Heart was $36 million, up 13%, and lung $5 million, down 2.5%.

As impressive as growth was in Q2, I think that margins stole the thunder. Gross margins were stable at peak levels of 61%, with product gross margins stable and service gross margins rising to record 33%. Meanwhile, operational leverage drove operating margin to 23%, leading to operating profit of $36 million, net income of $35 million, and EPS of $1.03.

The strong quarter pushed management to raise their sales guidance for the year by $20 million, now expecting $585 to $605 million, reflecting 35% growth at the midpoint, up from 30% in the Q1 guide.

CEO Is Buying Ahead Of Dreadful Q3

On August 11th, it was disclosed the CEO, Waleed H. Hassanein, bought 16,875 shares, at an average price of about $117, for a combined total of slightly less than $2 million. This sent shares to a 12% rise, which they mostly gave back since.

There's the old saying that insiders have plenty of reasons to sell but only one reason to buy, but I think some nuance is due here. This buy is for about 1% of CEO's total stake of about $200 million, so it's quite negligible from his view.

However, as evidenced by the 12% rise in stock, investors tend to receive such buys with strong enthusiasm.

In my mind, this is more of a move to give some confidence ahead of what will be another weak third quarter, as investors are still gauging the size of the seasonal decline they should expect.

Last year, sales declined 5% Q/Q. This year, consensus estimates reflect an even bigger 8% decline, which is supported by industry data.

The way I see it, it's hard to pinpoint investor expectations for Q3 right now, and I'm worried that those that expect another strong beat & raise will be disappointed.

Valuation

At $120 per share, TransMedics trades at 54 times forward earnings and 7 times forward sales. On 2026 estimates, it trades at 46 times and 6, respectively.

The way I see it, this valuation bakes in strong results in the near term. Mainly, it reflects expectations for revenue growth higher than the consensus 20% in 2026. In my view, this will primarily depend on contributions from the new lung and heart programs, which is an unknown at this point.

Conclusion

TransMedics long-term prospects remain attractive, and those with the stomach to withstand near-term volatility should be rewarded.

I don't like the setup heading into Q3, and CEO's share buying isn't enough to change my mind here, due to its insignificant size.

As such, I reiterate a 'Hold', waiting for a more attractive setup before upgrading.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2045 Lifetime Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • After a tumultuous start to the second quarter, the financial markets regained their footing and provided positive total returns for the full three months.
  • The portfolio delivered a gain and outperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation was the primary factor in the fund's relative strength.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington's new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop. Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lowerquality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation contributed to results, primarily as a result of our decision to maintain an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. An underweight in international stocks also made a small contribution. On the other hand, an overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute terms weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. An overweight in U.S. mid caps, which didn't keep pace with large caps, had a modest, negative effect on results. The fund's absolute return portfolio further detracted, as did underlying manager performance.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors' rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don't attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.

data.title: AAON: A Secular Growth Play Facing Current Challenges
data.description:
Shares of AAON, Inc. (NASDAQ:AAON) have become part of the momentum ride, which data center suppliers have benefited from in recent years. Shares, however, are down substantially from the peak, in part as the business still has large legacy HVAC operations but also as the business suffers from execution issues.

In between higher expectations amidst the sound positioning and some near-term execution issues, valuations are very demanding, and while the company will likely see spectacular profit growth in the near term, I fear that resulting performance will not provide a very compelling valuation at current levels amidst too much ambiguity out there today.

Not Your Typical HVAC Company

The paragraph header is how AAON describes its own business, claiming to be an entrepreneurial disruptor in the HVAC industry, driven by a focus on providing customer value, agility, and innovative practices.

Founded back in 1988, AAON operates two separate brand families, which each cater to a distinctive business line and applications. The namesake AAON brand focuses on the production and sale of rooftop units, handlers, and condensing units used in education, retail, manufacturing, and office applications.

BASX provides air handling, liquid cooling, and cleanroom environments for datacenters and their related cleanrooms. The business is mostly focused on the US, and the AAON namesake brand makes up the vast majority of sales, about three-quarters of total revenues, with BASX being the smaller unit, yet one seeing a 40% compounded annual growth rate over the past three years, while AAON is a much more stable and traditional business.

This creates an interesting dynamic, namely that of a growth gem in the business line, as this business becomes larger and thereby boosts the pace of organic growth of the entire business. This is clearly visible in the long-term performance of the business. Over the past decade, revenues have risen from just over $300 million to nearly $1.3 billion, all amidst a stable share count. All this has been reflected in the share price, as a $15 stock a decade ago now exchanges hands at levels in the mid-eighties.

Valuation Thoughts

Earlier this year, the company reported results for 2024, which really require an in-depth look to fully comprehend the trends taking place. Full-year sales were reported up nearly 3% to $1.20 billion, as GAAP operating profits were down 8% to $209 million, with GAAP earnings down 5% to $2.02 per share.

The company really suffered from struggles in the core AAON business, offset by strong growth in the BASX segment, with the latter being the driver behind a 70% increase in the year backlog to $867 million.

The company operated with about $150 million in net debt, no issue at all in light of a $272 million EBITDA number reported for the year, with these net debt levels being the result of a $213 million capital expenditure spent. This compares to just a $62 million combined depreciation and amortization charge incurred last year, indicating very large net capital expenditures being made, geared towards capacity expansion at BASX.

Needless to say, valuations were creeping up mostly because of fantasy around BASX as shares doubled to a high of $140 in 2024, which, given the earnings power just discussed, results in sky-high valuations. In all fairness, shares have fallen and have been trading in a $60-$100 range since the spring of the year.

Despite the uneven results for 2024, the company announced a $30 million buyback program in March and announced a 25% increase in the dividend, although the percentage hike is more impressive than the actual dividend, now payable at $0.40 per share each year.

Growth Comes Through

The company reported very interesting first quarter results, with total sales up 23% to $322 million, but amidst severe margin pressure, operating profits were down a quarter to $35 million, with adjusted earnings down 9 cents to $0.35 per share.

The interesting part is that while AAON segment sales were down 19% to $189 million, it were BASX sales, which were up no less than 375% to $132 million, now making up 40% of sales here. This momentum furthermore drove a huge increase in the backlog to $1.03 billion, up $160 million in the first quarter.

In Augusts, second quarter sales were posted flat at $311 million and change as the backlog rose further to $1.12 billion, driven by continued order intake at BASX. Results were impacted by ERP implementation issues and resulting supply chain disruptions. The reporting was a bit hard to read as BASX sales rose 20% to $68 million, but the reporting nature differs from the first quarter as the size of this segment is much different from first quarter numbers, but this might have to do with my unfamiliarity with the business.

Similar to the first quarter, company-wide margins continued to come down, with quarterly operating income reported at $23 million. As a result, earnings in the first half of the year are cut in half to $0.54 per share, which makes that earnings only trend around a dollar per share here, which is not really fair either as some of the current problems are really of short-term nature.

Due to net investments made into the business, net debt has risen to $317 million, something to take note of as EBITDA fell to $103 million for the first half of the year, driven by headwinds in margins. This results in a roughly 1.5 times leverage ratio, no issue, despite continued elevated capital expenditure needs.

What Now?

Right now, the 83 million shares of AAON trade at $85, granting equity a $7.0 billion valuation and a modestly higher enterprise valuation. This is applied to a business that generates about $1.3 billion in sales, mostly a struggling AAON namesake business and a promising BASX segment.

The issue is that earnings have taken a beating and are very modest in relation to the current valuations here, as profits around $2 per share in 2024 are set to come down meaningfully this year, only trending just over a dollar per share here. Even if current woes are accounted for and 2024 earnings return, this yields very demanding valuation multiples, too demanding to see appeal despite the promise around its positioning.

These near-term headwinds relate to challenged implementation of ERP systems, supply chain issues, and large investment requirements, while the long-term outlook remains good. This sound positioning does not rhyme with near-term margins, as the picture is a bit messy from this perspective, although expected to clear soon. Full-year sales are seen up double digits to around $1.35 billion, up around $150 million in dollar terms, as revenues year to date are up $58 million here.

So the question is what earnings will look like once the issues are resolved. A 15-20% operating margin target on $1.5 billion sales looks quite reasonable, which would translate into earnings around $2.50 per share at the high end of the range, still creating a demanding set-up here.

Amidst these hiccups and the foregoing discussion, I am cautious here because even if growth returns in the near term and margins recover, current earnings power is relatively modest in relation to the valuation here.

However, the positioning is very interesting to keep track of from here going forward, but unwilling to participate here or have conviction on the growth story.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2035 Lifetime Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • After a tumultuous start to the second quarter, the financial markets regained their footing and provided positive total returns for the full three months.
  • The portfolio delivered a gain and outperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation was the primary factor in the fund's relative strength.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington's new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop. Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lowerquality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation contributed to results, primarily as a result of our decision to maintain an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. An overweight in emergingmarket debt contributed, as well. On the other hand, the real assets portfolio detracted due to negative returns for energy stocks. An overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute terms—also weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. The fund's absolute return portfolio further detracted, as did underlying manager performance.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors' rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don't attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.
